summary: The Drummond Ranch was established by my grandfather, Frederick Drummond, in Osage County, Oklahoma. It became the foundation for our family’s involvement in cattle ranching, with my father and uncles each running their own independent ranches. The ranching business here thrived on leasing land from the Osage tribe and raising cattle that were driven up from Texas after the Civil War. Over the years, the ranch adapted with changes in transportation and cattle management, especially after World War II.

My grandfather, Frederick Drummond, originally came from Scotland in the early 1880s. He was a full-blood Scot who ventured to America seeking opportunities as an Indian trader. His journey took him through Texas and St. Louis before he settled in Osage County, Oklahoma, where he started the Drummond Ranch and trading company.

Texas was where my grandfather first tried to make his fortune as an Indian trader but unfortunately lost what little money he had. Later, cattle were driven from Texas across Oklahoma to Kansas railroads after the Civil War, grazing on the tall grass prairie in Osage County. My family’s ranching business was closely tied to these cattle drives originating in Texas, which supplied beef to the eastern United States.

While working his way back east after losing money in Texas, my grandfather stopped in St. Louis where he met Charlie Skinner, an Indian trader. This meeting was pivotal because Skinner invited him to trade in the Osage, which led my grandfather to settle in Osage County. St. Louis was a key waypoint in his journey to establishing the Drummond Ranch and trading business.

Charlie Skinner was an Indian trader I heard about who met my grandfather in St. Louis in the early 1880s. He offered my grandfather a job trading with him in the Osage, which was the opportunity that brought our family to Osage County. Without that chance encounter, my grandfather might not have settled here and started the Drummond Ranch.

Osage County is where my grandfather settled in the early 1880s and established the Drummond Ranch and the Hominy Trading Company. The county’s tall grass prairie was ideal for grazing cattle driven up from Texas after the Civil War. Our family leased land from the Osage tribe here and became one of the earliest white settlers involved in ranching. The area was central to the cattle industry, with railroads shipping beef to markets like Kansas City and St. Louis.

Frederick Drummond was my grandfather who came from Scotland in the early 1880s. He started as an Indian trader and settled in Osage County, Oklahoma, where he founded the Drummond Ranch and the Hominy Trading Company. He married a full-blood German woman from Coffeeville, Kansas, and had three sons and a daughter. Unfortunately, he died young at age forty-nine from appendicitis, but he laid the foundation for our family’s ranching legacy.

Coffeeville, Kansas is where my grandfather’s wife was from; she was a full-blood German. After marrying her, my grandfather traded in Pawhuska and then Hominy, Oklahoma. Coffeeville represents the family’s connections beyond Oklahoma and shows the diverse heritage that shaped the Drummond family.

Pawhuska was one of the early trading posts where my grandfather conducted business after marrying. It was part of the Osage County area where the Drummond family established themselves. The trading company later expanded to Hominy, becoming a central hub for supplies in the region.

Hominy, Oklahoma is where my grandfather and two partners founded the Hominy Trading Company, which was like the Walmart of Osage County at the time. The company supplied everything from groceries to hardware and even coffins. It was a vital resource for farmers and ranchers in the area and a cornerstone of our family’s business before ranching took precedence.

The Hominy Trading Company was founded by my grandfather and two other men in Hominy, Oklahoma. It was essentially the super Walmart of Osage County back then, offering everything from groceries to hardware and furniture. My father took over the company after my grandfather died, running it before eventually moving more into the ranching business. It was a major part of our family’s early economic foundation.

The American Civil War ended before my grandfather arrived, but it had a big impact on the cattle industry that shaped our ranching business. After the war, countless cattle were driven from Texas across Oklahoma to Kansas railroads for shipment east. This post-war cattle drive era was crucial for the development of ranching in Osage County and for families like mine.

The Chisholm Trail was a famous cattle trail west of Osage County, but cattle drives through Osage were more scattered without a specific trail. While the Chisholm Trail left visible wagon marks and was well known, the cattle drives in Osage County covered a broad area and crossed lands of the Cherokee and Choctaw Nations. Our ranching history is tied to these drives but not directly to the Chisholm Trail itself.

The Cherokee Nation’s land was part of the area cattle drives passed through on their way from Texas to Kansas. While Osage County was our main base, the cattle were scattered and crossed Cherokee and Choctaw lands as well. These tribal lands were integral to the cattle grazing routes during the post-Civil War era.

Like the Cherokee Nation, the Choctaw Nation’s lands were traversed by cattle drives moving from Texas to Kansas after the Civil War. The cattle were scattered over a large area including Choctaw land, which was part of the grazing grounds used en route to the railroads. This made the Choctaw Nation an important part of the regional cattle industry that supported our ranching business.

Fred G. Drummond was my father, who graduated from Oklahoma State at sixteen and then attended Harvard Business School. He came back home to run the Hominy Trading Company after my grandfather died young. Later, he became more involved in the cattle trading and ranching business, continuing the family legacy in Osage County until he died at age sixty-three.

Roy Cecil Drummond, or R.C., was my father’s older brother and the oldest son of Frederick Drummond. He was more the cowboy of the family, taking on the hands-on ranching and cattle work. While my father managed the trading company and later cattle trading, R.C. was deeply involved in the physical ranching side in Osage County.

Jack Drummond was one of my grandfather’s three sons involved in the cattle business. Like my father and brother, he ran his own independent ranch in Osage County. Each of the Drummond brothers managed their own operations, contributing to the family’s overall presence in the ranching industry.

Alfred Alexander Drummond, known as A.A., was another of my grandfather’s sons who became a rancher. He managed his own ranch in Osage County and was part of the trio of brothers who carried on the family’s cattle business. Each brother had their own independent ranch, though they were all part of the larger Drummond legacy.

My father attended Harvard Business School after graduating early from Oklahoma State. He was in the third business school class there but didn’t complete his degree because he returned home when my grandfather died. Harvard was important in shaping his business skills, which he used to run the Hominy Trading Company and later the cattle trading business.

My father graduated from Oklahoma State University when he was just sixteen, having been mostly home-schooled in Hominy. Education was very important in our family, and Oklahoma State was the first step for him before he went on to Harvard. The university played a key role in preparing him for his business career.

I earned my MBA from Stanford University after serving in the military in Germany during the 1950s. My education there gave me the skills to run the family ranching business effectively when I returned home. Although I initially pursued banking in Kansas City, Stanford prepared me well for managing the Drummond Ranches.

After completing my MBA at Stanford, I worked in banking in Kansas City, managing cattle loans. It was a different life from ranching, and I initially preferred it. However, when my father became ill with lung cancer, I returned to Kansas City and then back home to Osage County to take over the family ranching business.

During World War II, I was involved in physically demanding ranch work like cattle roundups, often starting before dawn and working in extreme heat. At about fourteen, I rode many miles on horseback and swore I would never be a rancher. The war years were tough on the cattle industry, with grass-fed beef being the norm since corn-fed beef wasn’t available then.

I served in the military in Germany during the 1950s before pursuing my MBA at Stanford. My time in Germany was part of my early adult years and shaped my perspective before returning to the family ranch. It was a significant chapter between my education and my eventual return to ranching in Oklahoma.

Sand Springs was home to the first stockyard near Tulsa, Oklahoma, where cattle were shipped by rail in the early to mid-20th century. The stockyards were critical for moving cattle from Osage County to markets like Kansas City and St. Louis. Over time, the stockyard moved to the east side of Tulsa, reflecting changes in the cattle industry.

Tulsa had a stockyard that was important for the cattle industry in Osage County and surrounding areas. It originally started in Sand Springs and later moved to the east side of Tulsa. The stockyards facilitated the shipment of cattle by rail and later by truck, which revolutionized how ranchers like us managed cattle sales.

Bristow was one of the smaller towns in Oklahoma that had a stockyard for cattle. Along with places like Pawnee and Coffeeville, it served as a local hub for moving cattle by truck after the railroads’ dominance declined. These stockyards helped modernize the cattle industry in the region during the mid-1900s.

Pawnee was another Oklahoma town with a stockyard that played a role in the cattle industry. Like Bristow and Coffeeville, it became a point where cattle were gathered and shipped by truck, reflecting the shift from rail to road transport. These towns were important for ranchers in Osage County and beyond during the 1950s and later.
